question: Which country holds the largest official gold reserves? (2026)
explain: 🥇 USA: 8,133 tonnes — 🥈 Germany: 3,352t — 🥉 Italy: 2,452t. The US has held the top spot since WWII. China, despite being the world's largest gold producer, ranks only 6th in reserves.

question: Chewing gum is famously restricted in ______.
explain: Singapore banned chewing gum in 1992 after the government spent millions cleaning gum off subway doors. Only therapeutic gum (nicotine, dental) is allowed with a doctor's prescription. Fines can reach $100,000 SGD.

question: France has more time zones than the United States.
explain: France has 12 time zones thanks to its overseas territories (French Polynesia, Guadeloupe, Réunion, etc.), making it the country with the most time zones in the world. The US has only 11.

question: Which country has the most pyramids in the world?
explain: Sudan has around 255 pyramids — nearly twice as many as Egypt (138). Built by the Nubian kingdom of Kush between 700 BC and 300 AD, they are narrower and steeper than Egyptian ones. Most tourists have never heard of them.

question: Which country was the first to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender?
explain: El Salvador made Bitcoin legal tender in September 2021 under President Bukele — a world first. The government distributed $30 in BTC to every citizen. By 2025, El Salvador holds around 6,000 BTC in its national reserve.

question: Which country has the most cattle in the world?
explain: Brazil has ~240 million head of cattle — more cattle than people (215M). It is the world's largest beef exporter. India has more cows in total but most are not raised for meat due to religious reasons.

question: Christopher Columbus was born in which city?
explain: Columbus was born in Genoa, Italy, around 1451. He sailed under the Spanish flag but was never Spanish. He made 4 voyages to the Americas (1492–1504) and never actually reached mainland North America — only Caribbean islands and Central/South America.

question: Which country has been involved in the most wars in the last 100 years?
explain: The United States has been involved in over 100 military interventions since 1945 alone. From Korea to Vietnam, Iraq, Afghanistan and beyond — the US has had troops in combat in more countries than any other nation in modern history.

question: Which country has the highest proportion of women in its population?
explain: Latvia has one of the most female-skewed populations on Earth: ~54% women. This is largely due to WWII losses and higher male mortality rates. Nepal also ranks high (54%) because many men emigrate for work, leaving women behind.

question: Which city receives the most sunshine hours per year?
explain: Yuma, Arizona (USA) is the sunniest city on Earth with ~4,015 sunshine hours per year — over 90% of daylight hours are sunny. Cairo comes in 2nd with ~3,500h. Dubai gets around 3,400h.

question: Australia is wider than the Moon.
explain: Australia measures about 4,000 km from east to west. The Moon's diameter is only 3,474 km. So yes — Australia is literally wider than the Moon. The Moon is smaller than most people imagine.

question: Which country has the most spoken languages?
explain: Papua New Guinea has over 840 languages — more than any country on Earth, representing ~12% of all world languages. Nigeria comes 2nd with ~520. PNG has a population of only 10 million but extreme tribal diversity.

question: Which country has the highest GDP per capita in the world? (2025)
explain: Luxembourg leads global GDP per capita at ~$135,000/year (IMF 2025). Singapore ($88k) and Norway ($82k) follow. Luxembourg's wealth comes from its status as Europe's top financial center — it hosts more investment funds than any EU country.

question: What is the driest place on Earth?
explain: The Atacama Desert in Chile/Peru averages less than 1mm of rain per year in its driest parts. Some weather stations there have never recorded rain. It's so arid that NASA uses it to test Mars rovers — the soil chemistry is nearly identical.

question: Russia spans 11 different time zones.
explain: Russia covers 11 time zones — from UTC+2 (Kaliningrad) to UTC+12 (Kamchatka). When it's 6am in Moscow, it's already midnight the next day on the Pacific coast. It's the country with the most time zones on a single landmass.

question: Which country has the highest income tax rate in the world?
explain: Ivory Coast (Côte d'Ivoire) has a top marginal income tax rate of 60%. Among developed nations, Denmark leads at 55.9%. Sweden and Finland sit around 52%. High taxes in Ivory Coast contrast sharply with limited public services — a major issue for residents.

question: Which country has the most islands?
explain: Sweden has ~221,000 islands — the most of any country. Norway comes 2nd with ~50,000. Indonesia (~17,000) and the Philippines (~7,600) are often cited but rank much lower. Most of Sweden's islands are tiny and uninhabited.

question: Which country has the lowest life expectancy in the world? (2025)
explain: Central African Republic has a life expectancy of ~55 years — the lowest globally (WHO 2025). Chad (~57) and Sierra Leone (~57) follow closely. For comparison, Japan leads at ~84 years. CAR suffers from extreme poverty, civil conflict and limited healthcare access.

question: Which is the most practiced religion in the world?
explain: Christianity is the world's largest religion with ~2.4 billion followers (31% of world population). Islam follows with ~1.9 billion (24%). However, Islam is the fastest-growing religion and is projected to surpass Christianity by 2075.

question: Which country receives the most international tourists per year?
explain: France consistently tops global tourism with ~100 million visitors/year — nearly 1.5x its own population. Paris alone draws ~40M/year. Spain (83M) and USA (77M) follow. France's lead is driven by food, fashion, culture and its central European location.

question: Where is the tallest building in the world located?
explain: The Burj Khalifa in Dubai (UAE) stands at 828 meters — the tallest building since 2010. The future Jeddah Tower in Saudi Arabia (1,000m+) is under construction and will surpass it. For context, the Eiffel Tower is only 330m tall.

question: The Sahara Desert was green and full of life just 5,000 years ago.
explain: Around 7,000–5,000 years ago, the Sahara had rivers, lakes, hippos and elephants — evidence found in rock paintings and fossils. A shift in Earth's orbital cycle reduced monsoon rainfall, turning it into desert. This period is called the 'Green Sahara' or African Humid Period.

question: Who holds the most Bitcoin in the world? (2025)
explain: Satoshi Nakamoto, Bitcoin's anonymous creator, is estimated to hold ~1 million BTC (never moved since 2010) worth ~$90B+ at 2025 prices. The US Government (~200,000 BTC, seized from criminals) and MicroStrategy (~450,000 BTC) follow. Satoshi's identity remains unknown.

question: Which city is currently the fastest growing in the world by population?
explain: Kinshasa (DR Congo) is growing faster than any megacity — projected to become Africa's largest city by 2030, surpassing Lagos. It grows by ~600,000 people per year. With ~17M residents today, it could reach 35M by 2050 according to UN projections.

question: Which two countries share the longest land border in the world?
explain: The US-Canada border stretches 8,891 km — the longest land border in the world, and remarkably, it's unfortified. The Russia-Kazakhstan border comes 2nd at 7,644 km. The US-Mexico border (3,145 km) is far shorter but far more fortified.

question: In which country was coffee first discovered and cultivated?
explain: Coffee was first discovered in Ethiopia (Kaffa region) around the 9th century. A goat herder named Kaldi noticed his goats became energetic after eating coffee berries. It spread to Yemen by the 15th century, then to Europe by the 17th. Today Brazil produces ~38% of world supply.

question: North Korea has no access to the global internet for ordinary citizens.
explain: North Koreans access a domestic intranet called 'Kwangmyong' with ~5,500 approved websites — no Google, no social media, no outside world. Only ~1,000 elite officials have real internet. The country has 24 IP addresses total. For comparison, Iceland has 1 million.

question: What is the name of the highest mountain in the world?
explain: Mount Everest (Nepal/China border) reaches 8,849 meters — measured precisely in 2020 by a joint China-Nepal survey. About 7,500 climbers have reached the summit since Hillary and Tenzing Norgay in 1953. Over 300 people have died attempting it.

question: Which country is the world's largest democracy?
explain: India is the world's largest democracy with ~970 million registered voters in 2024 general elections. The 2024 election lasted 44 days across 7 phases — the largest election ever organized in human history. India surpassed China as the world's most populous country in 2023.

question: The Amazon rainforest produces 20% of the world's oxygen.
explain: This is a popular myth. The Amazon produces large amounts of oxygen BUT also consumes roughly the same amount through decomposition — making it nearly carbon neutral on O2. The ocean produces ~50-80% of Earth's oxygen. Still, the Amazon is crucial for absorbing CO2 and regulating rainfall.

question: Which country has won the most Nobel Prizes?
explain: The USA leads with 411 Nobel Prizes (as of 2025) — more than the next 4 countries combined. The UK (140) and Germany (114) follow. The US dominates in Physics, Chemistry, Medicine and Economics. Most laureates are affiliated with just 10 elite universities.

question: What is the longest river in the world?
explain: The Nile (6,650 km) is generally accepted as the longest river, flowing through 11 countries from Burundi to Egypt. However, some geographers argue the Amazon (6,400 km) is longer depending on where the source is measured. The debate is still ongoing scientifically.

question: What percentage of the world's population speaks Mandarin Chinese as their first language?
explain: ~920 million people speak Mandarin as their mother tongue (12% of world population). However, English is the most widely spoken language overall when including second-language speakers (~1.5 billion). Spanish is the 2nd most spoken native language with ~490 million.

question: What is the coldest permanently inhabited place on Earth?
explain: Oymyakon (Russia) recorded -71.2°C in 1924 — the coldest temperature ever recorded for a permanently inhabited settlement. ~500 people live there. Phones die in minutes outside, eyelashes freeze, and cars are left running 24/7. Despite this, people have lived there for generations.

question: Which country has the most billionaires in the world? (2025)
explain: The US leads with ~900 billionaires (Forbes 2025), followed by China (~800) and India (~200). Elon Musk and Jeff Bezos consistently trade the top 2 spots globally. India is the fastest-growing country for new billionaires, adding ~25/year.

question: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid.
explain: Cleopatra died in 30 BC — roughly 2,500 years after the Great Pyramid was built (~2,560 BC). The Moon landing was in 1969, just ~2,000 years after her death. This mind-bending fact shows how ancient the pyramids really are — older to Cleopatra than she is to us.

question: Which country consumes the most coffee per capita?
explain: Finland consumes ~12 kg of coffee per person per year — the highest in the world. Scandinavian countries dominate the top 5 (Norway, Iceland, Denmark). Italians invented espresso but only rank ~13th. The US consumes the most in total volume but ranks low per capita.

question: Which country was the first to grant women the right to vote?
explain: New Zealand granted women the right to vote in 1893 — 27 years before the USA (1920) and 25 years before the UK (1918). Saudi Arabia only allowed women to vote in 2015. Today, women vote in every recognized country except Vatican City (where only cardinals vote).

question: By 2050, which continent will have the most French speakers?
explain: Africa already has ~60% of the world's French speakers (~360M people). By 2050, this could reach 85% as African populations boom. Countries like DR Congo, Madagascar and Senegal have French as official language. Africa will essentially become the new heartland of the French language.

question: The United Kingdom does not have a written constitution.
explain: The UK is one of only 3 countries without a written constitution (along with Israel and New Zealand). Its constitution is 'uncodified' — spread across laws, conventions and court decisions like the Magna Carta (1215), Bill of Rights (1689) and various Acts of Parliament.

question: Which country dumps the most plastic into the oceans?
explain: The Philippines contributes ~36% of ocean plastic globally — more than the next 4 countries combined (Oceana report 2021). Its 7,000+ islands and poor waste infrastructure make ocean disposal common. China, India and Brazil follow. The US contributes surprisingly little (~1%) due to better waste systems.

question: What is the smallest country in the world by area?
explain: Vatican City covers just 0.44 km² — entirely inside Rome. It has ~800 residents and its own bank, army (Swiss Guard), radio, railway and postal system. Despite its tiny size, the Pope's influence reaches 1.4 billion Catholics globally.

question: Which country is considered the most ethnically diverse in the world?
explain: Uganda is consistently ranked the most ethnically diverse country, with over 65 distinct ethnic groups and no single group exceeding 17% of the population. Chad and Cameroon follow closely. In contrast, countries like Japan (98% Japanese) and Korea are among the least diverse.

question: Which country contains the largest coral reef system in the world?
explain: Australia's Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth — 2,300 km long, visible from space, housing 1,500 fish species and 4,000 types of mollusk. It contributes ~$6.4 billion/year to the Australian economy. It has lost ~50% of its coral since 1995 due to bleaching.

question: Antarctica belongs to no country and has no permanent residents.
explain: Antarctica is governed by the Antarctic Treaty (1959), signed by 54 countries — no nation owns it. ~1,000-5,000 scientists live there seasonally but no permanent civilians. It's the only continent with no indigenous population, no government and no cities. It contains 70% of Earth's fresh water as ice.

question: Which country consumes the most alcohol per capita?
explain: Moldova tops alcohol consumption globally at ~15 liters of pure alcohol per person/year (WHO). Czech Republic (~14.5L), Latvia and Germany follow. Moldova's economy depends heavily on wine and brandy production. Russia, often assumed #1, ranks around 5th-7th depending on the year.

question: Vikings reached North America about 500 years before Christopher Columbus.
explain: Norse explorer Leif Erikson landed in 'Vinland' (now Newfoundland, Canada) around 1000 AD — confirmed by archaeological discovery at L'Anse aux Meadows in 1960. Columbus arrived in 1492. Despite this, Columbus's voyage had lasting impact because it led to permanent European colonization.

question: Which country has won the most Olympic medals in total history?
explain: The USA leads all-time with 2,640+ Olympic medals (Summer + Winter), including 1,061 gold. The Soviet Union (now defunct) comes 2nd with ~1,200. Despite having only 4% of the world's population, the US wins ~15-20% of all Olympic medals — largely due to funding and elite sports infrastructure.

question: Which country has the highest incarceration rate per capita?
explain: The USA incarcerates 639 people per 100,000 — the highest rate in the world. That's ~2.1 million prisoners, more than any country in absolute numbers too. For comparison: Norway has 57/100k, Japan 37/100k. The US has 4% of world population but 21% of world prisoners.

question: The Great Wall of China is visible from space with the naked eye.
explain: This is one of the most persistent myths. The Great Wall is only ~9 meters wide on average — far too narrow to see from space without aid. Multiple astronauts including China's own Yang Liwei confirmed they couldn't see it. You'd need a resolution equivalent to seeing a human hair from 2km away.

question: Which country has the most nuclear warheads? (2025)
explain: Russia has ~5,580 nuclear warheads — the most of any country (SIPRI 2025). The USA follows with ~5,044. Together they hold ~88% of all nuclear weapons on Earth. China is rapidly expanding its arsenal (~500 warheads) and may rival the US by 2030.

question: Which is the deepest lake in the world?
explain: Lake Baikal in Siberia (Russia) is 1,642 meters deep — the world's deepest lake. It also holds ~20% of Earth's unfrozen fresh surface water. At 25 million years old, it's also the world's oldest lake. It's home to the Baikal seal — the world's only freshwater seal species.

question: Japan has more vending machines per capita than any country in the world.
explain: Japan has ~5 million vending machines — 1 for every 23 people. They sell everything: ramen, umbrellas, ties, eggs, fresh flowers, sake, and even live crabs. Vending machine culture thrives because Japan is safe (machines rarely get vandalized) and people value convenience above all.

question: Which civilization first cultivated cacao and invented chocolate?
explain: The Olmecs (Mexico) were the first to cultivate cacao around 1500 BC — over 3,000 years ago. The Mayans later made it a sacred drink mixed with chili. The Aztecs used cacao beans as currency. Chocolate only arrived in Europe in the 16th century when Spanish conquistadors brought it back.

question: What is the most widely spoken language in Africa?
explain: Arabic is spoken by ~150 million Africans across North Africa — making it the most spoken language on the continent. French is official in 21 African countries. Swahili has ~200M speakers across East Africa but fewer native speakers. Africa has over 2,000 languages in total.

question: Canada has the longest coastline of any country in the world.
explain: Canada's coastline measures 202,080 km — the longest on Earth, more than the next 4 countries combined. It borders 3 oceans (Atlantic, Pacific, Arctic). For comparison, the USA has 19,924 km of coastline. Canada's coast is so vast that parts of it have never been mapped in detail.

question: What percentage of Earth's water is drinkable fresh water?
explain: Only 3% of Earth's water is fresh water — and 2.5% of that is frozen in glaciers and ice caps. That leaves just 0.5% accessible as liquid fresh water. Brazil, Russia and Canada hold ~40% of the world's usable fresh water. By 2040, over half the world's population could face water shortages.

question: Which city was ranked the most expensive in the world to live in? (2025)
explain: Zurich topped the EIU Cost of Living Index in 2024-2025, ahead of Singapore and Hong Kong. A meal out costs ~40 CHF, a monthly transport pass ~100 CHF. Average salary is high (~CHF 90,000/year) but so are taxes and rent. Geneva often swaps 1st/2nd place with Zurich.

question: Which country hosts the most refugees in the world? (2025)
explain: Turkey hosts ~3.2 million registered refugees — the most of any country (UNHCR 2025), mainly Syrians fleeing the civil war. Lebanon has the highest refugee-to-population ratio: 1 in 5 people is a refugee. Germany leads in Europe with ~2 million. There are currently ~117 million displaced people globally.

question: Finland consistently ranks as having one of the best education systems in the world despite starting school at age 7.
explain: Finnish children don't start formal school until age 7 — 2 years later than most countries. Yet Finland consistently tops global education rankings (PISA). There is little homework, no standardized tests until age 16, and teaching is a highly respected profession. The philosophy: childhood play is learning.

question: Which empire was the largest in history by land area?
explain: The British Empire at its peak (1920) covered 24% of Earth's land surface — 35.5 million km². It ruled over 412 million people (23% of world population). The Mongol Empire (13th century) was 2nd at 24 million km². Today the UK is just 244,000 km² — a remarkable contrast.

question: Approximately what percentage of the world's population has access to the internet? (2025)
explain: ~67% of the world (5.4 billion people) had internet access in 2025. Growth is slowing — the remaining 33% (~2.6B) mostly live in rural Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. North America and Europe are above 90%. Elon Musk's Starlink is specifically targeting remote areas to close this gap.

question: The Dead Sea is actually a lake, not a sea.
explain: The Dead Sea has no outlet — water flows in (from the Jordan River) but never out, only evaporating. This makes it a landlocked lake, not a sea. At 430 meters below sea level, it's the lowest point on Earth's surface. Its salt concentration (34%) is 10x saltier than the ocean — nothing can survive in it.

question: Which is the only Ancient Wonder of the World still standing today?
explain: The Great Pyramid of Giza (Egypt) is the only original Ancient Wonder of the World still standing — built around 2,560 BC, it remained the tallest man-made structure on Earth for 3,800 years until Lincoln Cathedral was built in 1311 AD. It was built with ~2.3 million stone blocks, some weighing 80 tonnes.

question: Which is the most spoken language in South America by number of speakers?
explain: Portuguese edges out Spanish in South America because Brazil — the continent's largest country with 215 million people — speaks Portuguese. Spanish has ~210 million speakers across 9 countries. Quechua, spoken by ~10 million Andean people, is the largest indigenous language and has co-official status in Peru and Bolivia.

question: Australia has more deadly animals than any other country.
explain: This is largely a myth. Australia does have many venomous species but very few deaths result — typically 0-2 people die from snake bites per year. India has 58,000 snake bite deaths annually. Brazil has the most venomous species by count. Australia's reputation for deadly animals is real but the actual death toll is extremely low.

question: Which country has the largest total forest area in the world?
explain: Russia has ~815 million hectares of forest — the most in the world (20% of all forests). Brazil comes 2nd (~497M ha), mostly the Amazon. Canada 3rd (~347M ha). However, Brazil loses the most forest annually to deforestation (~10,000 km²/year). Russia's forests absorb ~1 billion tonnes of CO2 per year.

question: In which country was the World Wide Web invented?
explain: Tim Berners-Lee, a British scientist, invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at CERN in Geneva, Switzerland. The internet (as a network) was created by ARPANET in the USA in 1969. These are two different things — the internet is the infrastructure, the Web is the system of web pages on top of it.

question: Which country experienced the worst hyperinflation in modern history?
explain: Hungary holds the record for worst inflation ever: in 1946, prices doubled every 15 hours. At peak, they printed 100 quintillion pengő notes. Zimbabwe (2008) doubled prices every 24 hours. Venezuela (2018) hit 1,000,000% annual inflation. Germany's 1923 hyperinflation, though famous, was actually less extreme than these.

question: Which country shares borders with the most other countries?
explain: Russia and China are tied with 14 neighboring countries each — the most of any nations. Russia borders: Norway, Finland, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Poland, Belarus, Ukraine, Georgia, Azerbaijan, Kazakhstan, Mongolia, China, North Korea. China borders: Russia, Mongolia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Afghanistan, Pakistan, India, Nepal, Bhutan, Myanmar, Laos, Vietnam, North Korea.

question: The Roman Empire fell over 1,500 years ago.
explain: The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 AD — that's ~1,550 years ago. However, the Eastern Roman Empire (Byzantine Empire) continued for another 1,000 years until 1453 AD when Constantinople fell to the Ottomans. So 'Rome' in some form lasted from ~753 BC to 1453 AD — over 2,200 years of continuous civilization.

question: Which country produces the most waste per capita?
explain: The USA generates ~800 kg of waste per person per year — the highest in the world. Germany generates ~600 kg but recycles 67% of it. Japan generates only ~350 kg with elaborate sorting systems. The average American throws away their own body weight in trash every month. The US produces 12% of global waste with 4% of world population.

question: What is the world's oldest operating university?
explain: The University of Bologna (Italy) was founded in 1088 — making it the oldest continuously operating university in the world. Oxford followed in 1096. Al-Azhar in Cairo (970 AD) is older but focuses on Islamic theology. Harvard, the oldest in the Americas, was founded in 1636 — nearly 550 years after Bologna.

question: What is the longest commercial non-stop flight route in the world?
explain: Singapore Airlines Flight SQ24 (Newark–Singapore) covers 15,350 km in ~18 hours — the longest non-stop commercial flight. It uses an Airbus A350-900ULR (Ultra Long Range). The plane burns ~200 tonnes of fuel on the journey. For context, Earth's circumference is 40,075 km — this flight covers 38% of it.

question: Which country has the most millionaires in the world? (2025)
explain: The USA has ~22 million millionaires (in USD) — the most of any country (Credit Suisse 2025). China has ~6 million. The UK and Germany each have ~3 million. However, Switzerland has the highest rate per capita: 15% of adults are millionaires. Monaco leads per capita with ~30%.

question: Which country has the most UNESCO World Heritage Sites?
explain: Italy leads with 58 UNESCO World Heritage Sites (2024) — from the Colosseum to Venice, Pompeii and the Amalfi Coast. China is 2nd with 57. Germany has 52, France 52 and Spain 50. Italy's sites span 3,000 years of history and represent ~5% of all UNESCO sites worldwide.

question: Which country receives the most immigrants annually?
explain: The USA admits ~1 million legal permanent residents (green cards) per year — more than any country. Germany (~700k), UK (~500k) and Canada (~450k) follow. However, per capita, Luxembourg and Switzerland receive far more immigrants relative to their population. Nearly 20% of all immigrants in the world live in the USA.

question: How much of the world's species biodiversity does the Amazon rainforest contain?
explain: The Amazon contains over 10% of all species on Earth — including 40,000 plant species, 1,300 bird species, 3,000 fish species and 2.5 million insect species. A new species is discovered there roughly every 2 days. It covers 5.5 million km² — an area larger than the entire European Union.

question: Iceland was once heavily forested before humans arrived.
explain: When Norse Vikings arrived (~874 AD), Iceland was covered by birch forests across 25-40% of its land. Within centuries, settlers cleared almost all trees for fuel and farmland. Today only ~2% of Iceland is forested. The country is now actively planting millions of trees to reverse 1,000 years of deforestation.

question: Which city has the most skyscrapers in the world (buildings over 150m)?
explain: Hong Kong has over 550 buildings taller than 150m — more than any city on Earth. New York has ~300, Shanghai ~280, Dubai ~200. Hong Kong's density is extreme: 7.5 million people on 1,100 km². The city's skyline is so dramatic that the Hong Kong Tourism Board runs dedicated skyline viewing tours.

question: What is the world's oldest known written language?
explain: Sumerian cuneiform (Mesopotamia, modern Iraq) dates to ~3,400 BC — the world's oldest known writing system. It began as pictographs to record trade goods and evolved into a complex script. Egyptian hieroglyphs emerged ~3,100 BC. Chinese writing appeared ~1,200 BC. Sanskrit's oldest text, the Rigveda, dates to ~1,500 BC.

question: Which country has the highest road fatality rate per capita?
explain: Thailand has ~32 deaths per 100,000 people per year — among the highest in the world (WHO). It is nicknamed 'the most dangerous country to drive in Asia.' Libya and Zimbabwe also rank very high. Norway and Sweden have the lowest rates (~2/100k). Road accidents kill ~1.35 million people globally per year — more than HIV/AIDS.

question: The Sahara Desert is larger than the continental United States.
explain: The Sahara covers ~9.2 million km² — nearly the same size as the USA (9.8 million km²) or China (9.6 million km²). It stretches across 11 countries. Despite being the world's largest hot desert, the Sahara is not the largest desert — Antarctica (~14 million km²) holds that title as it receives almost no precipitation.

question: Which country's workers put in the most hours per year on average?
explain: Mexico averages ~2,255 work hours per person per year (OECD 2024) — the highest among OECD nations. South Korea (~1,920h) and Japan (~1,700h) are often cited but Mexico leads. Germany averages just ~1,340h. Despite working the most, Mexican workers earn far less than their European counterparts — long hours don't equal productivity.

question: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest living structure on Earth.
explain: The Great Barrier Reef (Australia) stretches 2,300 km and is the world's largest living structure, composed of ~2,900 individual reefs and 900 islands. It's the only living thing visible from space. Since 1995, it has lost ~50% of its coral due to ocean warming and bleaching events. At current rates, 90% could disappear by 2050.

question: Which country sent the first woman to space?
explain: Valentina Tereshkova (USSR) became the first woman in space on June 16, 1963 — 20 years before the USA sent their first woman (Sally Ride, 1983). Tereshkova orbited Earth 48 times over 3 days. She was a textile factory worker and amateur skydiver with no pilot training before being selected.

question: Which country has the lowest population density in the world?
explain: Mongolia has just 2 people per km² — the lowest population density of any sovereign nation. Its 3.4 million people live in an area larger than Western Europe. Australia (3/km²) and Iceland (3/km²) follow closely. Despite this, ~45% of Mongolians live in the capital Ulaanbaatar — one of the most extreme urban concentrations.

question: Nepal is home to 8 of the world's 10 highest mountains.
explain: Nepal contains 8 of the 10 highest peaks on Earth including Everest (8,849m), Kangchenjunga (8,586m) and Lhotse (8,516m). The other 2 in the top 10 are K2 and Nanga Parbat — both in Pakistan. Nepal's Himalayas formed when the Indian tectonic plate collided with the Eurasian plate ~50 million years ago.

question: Which country was the first to abolish slavery entirely?
explain: Haiti abolished slavery in 1804 after the Haitian Revolution — the only successful slave uprising in history. Britain abolished the slave trade in 1807 but kept slaves in colonies until 1834. The USA abolished slavery in 1865 (13th Amendment). Brazil was the last in the Americas to abolish it, in 1888.

question: Russia sold Alaska to the United States for less than $20 million.
explain: Russia sold Alaska to the USA in 1867 for $7.2 million — just $0.02 per acre. At the time, critics called it 'Seward's Folly' after Secretary of State William Seward who negotiated the deal. Alaska turned out to contain vast oil reserves, gold, and fisheries worth trillions. It's now the largest US state by area.

question: Who was the longest-reigning monarch in recorded history?
explain: Sobhuza II of Swaziland (now Eswatini) reigned for 82 years and 254 days (1899–1982) — the longest verified reign in history. Louis XIV of France reigned 72 years. Queen Elizabeth II reigned 70 years (the longest of any female monarch). Sobhuza came to power as an infant and died at ~83 years old.

question: Which country has the highest rate of civilian gun ownership?
explain: The USA has ~120 guns per 100 people — the highest rate in the world. That's ~400 million civilian guns for 330 million people — more guns than people. Yemen (53/100) and Switzerland (28/100) follow. Switzerland requires military service and guns are kept at home, contributing to its rate. US gun deaths total ~45,000/year.

question: The concept of zero was invented in India.
explain: Indian mathematician Brahmagupta formalized the concept of zero and its mathematical rules in 628 AD. The Babylonians used a placeholder zero earlier, but India gave zero its identity as a standalone number. Without zero, modern mathematics, computing and physics would be impossible. It's arguably the most important invention in human history.

question: Which country has the highest percentage of vegetarians?
explain: India has ~38-40% of its population following a vegetarian diet — by far the highest of any large country. This is driven by Hindu, Jain and Buddhist religious traditions. Israel has ~13% vegetarians (highest per capita outside India). The UK has ~12%. The global average is only ~8%.

question: What is the world's highest capital city?
explain: La Paz, Bolivia sits at 3,640 meters above sea level — the world's highest capital city. The air is so thin that athletes need weeks to acclimatize. La Paz even has the world's largest urban cable car network (Mi Teleférico) to navigate its steep terrain. Sucre is Bolivia's constitutional capital but La Paz is the seat of government.

question: Earth is not a perfect sphere — it is slightly flattened at the poles.
explain: Earth is an oblate spheroid — it bulges at the equator and is flattened at the poles due to its rotation. The equatorial diameter is 12,756 km while the polar diameter is 12,714 km — a 42 km difference. This also means Mount Chimborazo in Ecuador, not Everest, is the point furthest from Earth's center.

question: Which country consumes the most cheese per capita?
explain: France consumes ~27 kg of cheese per person per year — the most in the world. Denmark and Iceland follow at ~26 kg. France has over 1,000 named cheese varieties — Charles de Gaulle famously said 'How can you govern a country that has 246 types of cheese?' The French eat cheese after every meal as a course.

question: Which country is often considered the world's oldest continuous civilization?
explain: China is generally considered the world's oldest continuous civilization with recorded history going back ~4,000 years. The Xia dynasty (~2070 BC) is the earliest Chinese dynasty in historical records. Iran (Persia, 550 BC as empire) and Egypt (~3100 BC unified) are also strong candidates depending on how 'continuous' is defined.

question: North and South Korea are technically still at war.
explain: The Korean War (1950-1953) ended with an armistice — a ceasefire agreement — not a peace treaty. The two Koreas are therefore technically still at war, 70+ years later. The Korean Demilitarized Zone (DMZ), 4 km wide, is one of the most heavily militarized borders on Earth with ~2 million troops on both sides.

question: Which country has the most Spanish speakers in the world?
explain: Mexico has ~130 million Spanish speakers — more than Spain itself (~47M). The USA is actually 2nd worldwide with ~60 million Spanish speakers, making it larger than most Latin American countries. Spanish is the 2nd most spoken native language globally with ~490 million speakers across 21 countries.

question: Diamonds are actually extremely rare in nature.
explain: Diamonds are not geologically rare — billions of carats exist underground. Their perceived rarity is largely a marketing construct. De Beers, which controlled ~85% of world supply in the 20th century, artificially restricted supply to keep prices high. The famous 'A Diamond is Forever' campaign (1947) is considered the most successful ad campaign in history.

question: Which country consumes the most tea per capita?
explain: Turkey consumes ~3.5 kg of tea per person per year — the highest in the world. The average Turk drinks 10+ cups daily. Ireland (2.2 kg) and UK (1.9 kg) follow. China produces the most tea (~46% of world supply) but its per capita consumption is lower than these countries. Tea was first cultivated in China ~5,000 years ago.

question: What is the deepest point in the world's oceans?
explain: The Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench (Pacific Ocean) reaches 10,935 meters — the deepest known point on Earth. If you placed Mount Everest (8,849m) at the bottom, there would still be 2 km of water above it. Only 3 people have ever reached the bottom: Jacques Piccard (1960), James Cameron (2012) and Victor Vescovo (2019).

question: The Sahara is not the hottest desert on Earth.
explain: The Lut Desert in Iran recorded 70.7°C surface temperature in 2005 — the highest ever measured on Earth's surface (NASA). The Sahara's highest recorded air temperature was 58°C (Libya, 1922). Surface and air temperatures are different measurements. Death Valley holds the record for highest reliably recorded air temperature: 56.7°C (1913).

question: How many officially recognized languages does India have?
explain: India has 22 officially recognized languages in its constitution, including Hindi, Bengali, Tamil, Telugu and Urdu. In total, India has ~1,600 distinct languages and dialects. Hindi is spoken by only ~44% of Indians as a first language. English is the de facto language of government, education and business across the country.

question: Who discovered penicillin, the world's first antibiotic?
explain: Alexander Fleming (Scotland) discovered penicillin in 1928 by accident — he noticed mold killing bacteria in a petri dish he'd left out. It wasn't mass-produced until WWII (1940s). Penicillin has since saved an estimated 200 million lives. Fleming, Florey and Chain shared the 1945 Nobel Prize for this discovery.

question: Vatican City has its own military force.
explain: The Pontifical Swiss Guard, founded in 1506, is the world's oldest and smallest active military unit — just 135 soldiers. Only Swiss Catholic men aged 19-30 can join. They guard the Pope and Vatican entrances. Their Renaissance-era uniforms (designed by Michelangelo according to myth) make them the most recognizable soldiers on Earth.

question: In which direction is Arabic written?
explain: Arabic is written right to left — as are Hebrew, Persian, Urdu and Pashto. Books open from the 'back' by Western standards. Arabic has 28 letters, all consonants — vowels are indicated by diacritical marks. It's the liturgical language of Islam, spoken by ~375 million people across 22 countries.

question: Which country has the largest proven oil reserves?
explain: Venezuela holds the world's largest proven oil reserves at ~303 billion barrels (BP 2024) — ahead of Saudi Arabia (~267B) and Iran (~209B). However, Venezuela's oil is heavy crude, expensive to extract, while Saudi oil is light and cheap to pump. Despite massive reserves, Venezuela's oil output has collapsed due to political crisis and sanctions.

question: Which is the most active volcano in the world?
explain: Kilauea (Hawaii, USA) is considered the world's most active volcano — it has been erupting almost continuously since 1983, making it 40+ years of near-constant activity. Stromboli (Italy) has been erupting for ~2,000 years and is called the 'Lighthouse of the Mediterranean.' Etna is Europe's tallest and most active volcano at 3,357m.

question: More countries drive on the left side of the road than on the right.
explain: About 163 countries drive on the right, vs. only 76 on the left. However, ~35% of the world's population drives on the left — mostly former British colonies (UK, India, Australia, South Africa, Japan). Sweden switched from left to right overnight in 1967. Japan was never colonized yet drives on the left — likely influenced by British railway engineers.

question: Which country has the fastest average internet speed in the world? (2025)
explain: Singapore consistently leads global internet speeds at ~300 Mbps average (Ookla Speedtest 2025). South Korea and UAE follow closely. The global average is ~100 Mbps. The USA averages ~235 Mbps. Cuba has one of the world's slowest average speeds at ~4 Mbps. Internet speed strongly correlates with economic productivity.

question: What is the shortest war in history?
explain: The Anglo-Zanzibar War on August 27, 1896 lasted between 38 and 45 minutes — the shortest war in recorded history. Britain fired on the Sultan's palace after an unauthorized sultan took power; he surrendered almost immediately. The new sultan fled to German East Africa. British casualties: 1 wounded. Zanzibar casualties: ~500.

question: Which tiny country has won the most Olympic medals per capita?
explain: Liechtenstein (population ~38,000) has won 10 Olympic medals — all in Alpine skiing — giving it one of the highest medal rates per capita. Grenada (population ~113,000) won gold in 400m at the 2012 Olympics — the most impressive medal relative to population size. Jamaica consistently punches above its weight in sprinting.

question: Where on Earth does lightning strike the most frequently?
explain: Lake Maracaibo in Venezuela experiences lightning ~300 nights per year and up to 40,000 strikes per night — nicknamed 'Catatumbo lightning.' The phenomenon is caused by unique geography: cool Andes winds meeting warm lake air. It's so consistent that sailors used it as a natural lighthouse for centuries. It was even mentioned in a 16th-century epic poem.

question: The Great Wall of China is a single continuous wall.
explain: The Great Wall is not one wall but a series of walls, trenches, and fortifications built over 2,000+ years by different dynasties. The total length of all sections is ~21,196 km. Many sections are ruins or have disappeared entirely. Construction began in the 7th century BC and the last major work was done by the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644).

question: Switzerland has 4 official national languages. Which is the most spoken?
explain: German (Swiss German dialect) is spoken by ~63% of Swiss people. French is spoken by ~23% (mainly in the west, called 'Romandy'). Italian by ~8% (mainly Ticino). Romansh, an ancient Latin-derived language, is spoken by only ~0.5% (~36,000 people). Despite this diversity, Switzerland functions with remarkable unity.

question: Where did the world's first civilization emerge?
explain: Mesopotamia (modern Iraq/Syria), specifically Sumer, is generally considered the world's first civilization — emerging around 3,500 BC. It gave us writing, the wheel, the first legal code (Hammurabi), astronomy, and the 60-minute hour. The Indus Valley civilization (3,300 BC) and Egypt (~3,100 BC) developed around the same era.

question: Which country gets the highest percentage of its electricity from renewable energy?
explain: Iceland generates ~100% of its electricity from renewables — ~70% geothermal, ~30% hydropower. It's used for cheap aluminum smelting and Bitcoin mining. Norway generates ~98% from renewables (mostly hydro). Costa Rica regularly runs on 100% renewables for months at a time. Germany, despite massive investment, still gets ~46% from renewables.

question: The Amazon River discharges more water into the ocean than the next 7 largest rivers combined.
explain: The Amazon discharges ~209,000 cubic meters per second into the Atlantic — 20% of all freshwater flowing into Earth's oceans. The next 7 largest rivers combined discharge less. Its outflow is so massive that fresh water can be found 160 km into the Atlantic Ocean. The Amazon basin drains an area the size of the continental USA.

question: Which country has the most UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage elements?
explain: China leads with 43 UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage elements — including Peking opera, acupuncture, the Dragon Boat Festival and Tai Chi. Japan has 22, France 23. These are living traditions: crafts, performing arts, festivals and oral traditions. UNESCO created this list in 2003 to protect non-physical cultural heritage.

question: Which country has the most dams in the world?
explain: China has ~87,000 large dams — more than the rest of the world combined. The Three Gorges Dam on the Yangtze River is the world's largest power station by installed capacity (22,500 MW) and displaced ~1.4 million people during construction. China generates ~30% of global hydroelectric power. The USA is 2nd with ~9,000 dams.

question: Which country is made up of the most islands?
explain: Indonesia consists of ~17,000 islands — 6,000 are inhabited, making it the world's largest archipelago nation. It spans 5,000 km from east to west — roughly the width of the USA. With 270 million people across 5 main islands, Indonesia is the 4th most populous country and the world's largest Muslim-majority nation.

question: Roman soldiers were sometimes paid in salt, which is the origin of the word 'salary'.
explain: The Latin word 'salarium' (salary) is indeed linked to 'sal' (salt). Roman soldiers received a salt allowance or money to buy salt — an essential preservative before refrigeration. The phrase 'worth his salt' comes from this era. Salt was so valuable it was traded ounce-for-ounce with gold in parts of West Africa.

question: What is the coldest temperature ever recorded on Earth?
explain: The coldest temperature ever recorded on Earth's surface was -94.7°C, measured by satellite in East Antarctica in 2018. The coldest directly measured ground temperature was -89.2°C at Vostok Station (1983). For context, Mars averages -60°C. At -89°C, exposed skin freezes in seconds and steel becomes brittle as glass.

question: Which ocean basin generates the most tropical cyclones per year?
explain: The Western Pacific generates ~30 typhoons per year — the most of any ocean basin, accounting for ~1/3 of all tropical cyclones globally. The Philippines gets hit by ~20 typhoons/year — more than any country. Typhoon Haiyan (2013) was the strongest tropical cyclone ever recorded at landfall with 315 km/h winds.

question: The Saharan silver ant can survive temperatures above 70°C — more than any other animal on Earth.
explain: The Saharan silver ant (Cataglyphis bombycina) forages at surface temperatures up to 70°C — the highest heat tolerance of any known animal. They wait until the peak of midday heat when their lizard predators retreat, dash out for 10 minutes max to collect dead insects, then return to their cool underground nest. Their silver hairs reflect sunlight like a mirror.

question: Which country has the highest murder rate in the world? (2024)
explain: Jamaica had a murder rate of ~52 per 100,000 people in 2024 — among the highest globally. Honduras (~35/100k) and Venezuela (~33/100k) follow. El Salvador, once the world's most dangerous country, dramatically reduced its murder rate to ~2.4/100k by 2024 under President Bukele's mass incarceration policy — a record drop.

question: What is the oldest known living tree on Earth?
explain: Methuselah, a Great Basin bristlecone pine in California's White Mountains, is ~4,855 years old — making it the oldest known living individual tree. Its exact location is kept secret to protect it. It was already 2,800 years old when Rome was founded. The oldest clonal organism is Pando — a colony of quaking aspens in Utah estimated at 80,000 years old.

question: Which country has the most museums in the world?
explain: The USA has ~35,000 museums — more than any country, including ~17,500 history museums, ~1,000 art museums and ~250 children's museums. More Americans visit museums than attend major league sports events combined each year. The Smithsonian Institution alone has 19 museums in Washington DC — all free to visit.

question: Which country has the most active volcanoes?
explain: Indonesia has ~130 active volcanoes — the most of any country. It sits on the 'Ring of Fire' where 4 tectonic plates meet. The 1883 eruption of Krakatoa (Indonesia) was heard 4,800 km away and caused a global temperature drop of 1.2°C for 5 years. The 1815 Tambora eruption caused the 'Year Without a Summer' in 1816 — crop failures across the Northern Hemisphere.

question: China's population started declining for the first time in recorded history in 2023.
explain: China's population fell by ~850,000 in 2022 — the first decline in 60+ years. India overtook China as the world's most populous country in 2023 with ~1.43 billion people. China's one-child policy (1980–2015) created a demographic crisis: an aging population with too few young workers. China now encourages couples to have 3+ children.

question: Which country produces the most coffee in the world?
explain: Brazil produces ~38% of the world's coffee — more than the next 3 countries combined. Vietnam (16%) is 2nd, Colombia (8%) 3rd. Brazil produces mostly Arabica and Robusta beans across 27,000 km² of plantations. Ethiopia is the origin of coffee and still produces the most diverse wild varieties, considered the highest quality by connoisseurs.

question: Which country records the most shark attacks per year?
explain: The USA records ~50-60% of all global shark attacks annually — mainly in Florida (New Smyrna Beach, 'shark attack capital of the world'). Australia has more fatal attacks per incident. Worldwide there are only ~70-80 unprovoked attacks per year. You're 75x more likely to be killed by lightning than by a shark.

question: Antarctica is technically the world's largest desert.
explain: A desert is defined by precipitation, not heat. Antarctica receives less than 200mm of precipitation per year — making it technically the world's largest desert at 14 million km². The Sahara (9.2M km²) is the largest hot desert. The Antarctic Dry Valleys receive virtually no precipitation and are the closest environment on Earth to Mars.

question: Which country experiences the 'midnight sun' — daylight for 24 hours — for the most days per year?
explain: Norway's Svalbard archipelago experiences 24-hour daylight for ~4 months (April–August) and polar night for ~4 months. The sun doesn't set for 126 consecutive days. In contrast, during polar winter the sun doesn't rise for months. Norway is nicknamed 'Land of the Midnight Sun' — you can read a book outside at 2am in summer.

question: Which country was the first to fully legalize cannabis nationally?
explain: Uruguay became the first country to fully legalize recreational cannabis in 2013 under President José Mujica — a former guerrilla who lived on a farm and donated 90% of his presidential salary to charity. Canada followed in 2018. The Netherlands has famously tolerated cannabis in coffeeshops since the 1970s but never fully legalized it.

question: Which country has the highest national debt as a percentage of GDP?
explain: Japan's national debt is ~260% of GDP (2025) — the highest of any large economy. Despite this, Japan is not in crisis because it owes most of its debt to its own citizens and institutions. Greece (~180%), Italy (~145%) and the USA (~123%) follow. Japan's debt situation is unique and unlikely to trigger default due to internal ownership.

question: Which country has the world's oldest continuously used national flag?
explain: Denmark's Dannebrog (a white cross on red) has been in continuous use since ~1370 AD — the world's oldest national flag still in use. According to legend, it fell from the sky during the Battle of Lyndanisse in 1219. The Union Jack (UK) dates to 1606. The Netherlands flag (1572) and Switzerland's (1339) are also ancient.

question: Which company or country operates the most satellites in orbit? (2025)
explain: SpaceX's Starlink constellation has over 6,000 active satellites in low Earth orbit (2025) — more than all other operators combined. The total number of active satellites from all operators is ~10,000. Before Starlink launched in 2019, there were only ~2,000 active satellites. Starlink provides internet to ~3 million subscribers in 60+ countries.

question: Nepal is the only country in the world with a non-rectangular national flag.
explain: Nepal's flag consists of two stacked pennants — creating a unique double-triangle shape. It's the only non-quadrilateral national flag in use. The crimson red represents bravery, the blue border peace, the moon cooler weather, and the sun fierce resolve. Nepal officially codified its unique flag shape with precise mathematical instructions in its 1962 constitution.

question: Which country has the most Portuguese speakers outside of Brazil and Portugal?
explain: Angola has ~35 million Portuguese speakers — the 3rd largest Portuguese-speaking population after Brazil (~215M) and Portugal (~10M). Mozambique follows with ~33M. Portuguese is the fastest-growing European language in Africa due to population booms in former Portuguese colonies. By 2050, Africa will have more Portuguese speakers than South America.

question: The Pacific Ocean is larger than all of Earth's landmasses combined.
explain: The Pacific Ocean covers ~165 million km² — larger than all continents combined (~149 million km²). It contains more than half of Earth's ocean water. The distance from Indonesia to Colombia across the Pacific is ~19,800 km — nearly half the circumference of the Earth. The Pacific is so vast it has its own timezone region.

question: Which passport grants access to the most countries visa-free? (2025)
explain: Singapore's passport ranked #1 in 2025 with visa-free or visa-on-arrival access to ~195 countries (Henley Passport Index). Japan and Germany follow closely at 193-194. An Afghan passport grants access to only ~28 countries — the weakest in the world. The difference between the strongest and weakest passport represents an enormous privilege gap.

question: Which city has the most expensive average rent in the world? (2025)
explain: Hong Kong consistently ranks as the most expensive city for rent globally — a 50m² apartment averages ~$4,500/month. New York (~$4,200), San Francisco (~$3,800) and London (~$3,500) follow. Hong Kong's extreme prices are driven by its tiny land area (1,100 km²) housing 7.5 million people, with mountains limiting buildable land to ~25%.

question: Which country elected the world's first female president?
explain: Iceland elected Vigdís Finnbogadóttir in 1980 — the world's first democratically elected female head of state. She served 4 terms until 1996. Indira Gandhi (India, 1966) and Sirimavo Bandaranaike (Sri Lanka, 1960) were earlier female leaders but as Prime Ministers, not Presidents. Argentina's Isabel Perón (1974) technically preceded Iceland but inherited power rather than being elected directly.

question: What is the most visited paid monument in the world?
explain: The Forbidden City in Beijing receives ~17 million visitors per year — the most of any monument. It is capped at 80,000 visitors per day. The Colosseum draws ~7 million/year, the Eiffel Tower ~7 million. The Great Wall (Mutianyu section) gets ~10 million. The Forbidden City was home to 24 Chinese emperors over 500 years and has 9,999 rooms.

question: Which country has the most unicorn startups (valued over $1 billion)? (2025)
explain: The USA has ~700+ unicorn startups — more than the rest of the world combined (CB Insights 2025). China has ~350, India ~110, UK ~50. Silicon Valley alone produces more unicorns than most countries. The USA's dominance comes from its deep venture capital ecosystem, top universities and culture of risk-taking.

question: What is the longest mountain range in the world?
explain: The Andes stretch 7,000 km along South America's western coast — the world's longest continental mountain range. They run through 7 countries from Venezuela to Chile/Argentina. The mid-ocean ridge system is technically longer (65,000 km) but lies mostly underwater. The Andes contain Aconcagua (6,961m) — the highest peak outside Asia.

question: Which city has the most billionaires in the world? (2025)
explain: New York City has ~119 billionaires (Forbes 2025) — the most of any city. Beijing (111) and Hong Kong (71) follow. London has ~50 billionaire residents. Mumbai is India's billionaire capital with ~92. Despite NYC leading in count, Monaco has the highest density — ~1 billionaire per 1,000 residents.

question: There is no land beneath the North Pole — it is floating on the Arctic Ocean.
explain: The North Pole sits on Arctic Ocean water covered by sea ice — there is no landmass beneath it. This contrasts sharply with the South Pole, which sits on Antarctica, a continent 2,835m above sea level. Because of this, the North Pole has no permanent research station — only temporary camps on drifting ice. The Arctic sea ice is shrinking by ~13% per decade.

question: Which book has been the most translated in history after the Bible?
explain: The Little Prince by Antoine de Saint-Exupéry has been translated into ~300 languages — the most of any non-religious book. Over 200 million copies have been sold since 1943, making it one of the best-selling books ever. Don Quixote (~500 translations) and Pinocchio are close rivals. Saint-Exupéry disappeared over the Mediterranean in 1944 while on a reconnaissance mission.

question: Which country has the world's oldest continuous monarchy?
explain: Japan's imperial dynasty is the world's oldest continuous monarchy — Emperor Naruhito is the 126th emperor of an unbroken line dating to ~660 BC (though this early history is legendary). The Japanese imperial family has ruled for at least 1,500 documented years. In contrast, the British monarchy dates to 1066 — ~1,000 years. Japan's emperor is now a ceremonial figurehead with no political power.

question: Switzerland exports more watches by value than all other countries combined.
explain: Switzerland exports ~$26 billion worth of watches annually (2024) — more than 50% of global watch export value, despite Switzerland having just 0.1% of world population. Top brands include Rolex, Patek Philippe, Omega and Swatch. A single Patek Philippe watch sold for $31 million in 2019 — the most expensive watch ever auctioned. Swiss watchmaking employs ~60,000 people.

question: Which country has won the most FIFA World Cups?
explain: Brazil has won 5 World Cups (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002) — the most of any nation. Germany and Italy have 4 each. Argentina won their 3rd in 2022 with Messi, drawing level with Germany and Italy in 3rd place. Brazil is the only country to have participated in every single World Cup since 1930.

question: An area of Amazon rainforest the size of a soccer field is destroyed every single minute.
explain: According to WWF and INPE data, approximately 1-2 football fields of Amazon rainforest are cleared every minute. That's ~10,000 km² per year in Brazil alone. Since 1970, about 20% of the Amazon has been destroyed. The rainforest creates its own rainfall cycle — scientists warn that at 25% deforestation, the entire ecosystem could collapse irreversibly.

question: What is the world's highest waterfall?
explain: Angel Falls in Venezuela drops 979 meters — the world's highest uninterrupted waterfall, nearly 20x taller than Niagara Falls (57m). It was 'discovered' by American aviator Jimmie Angel in 1933. Victoria Falls in Zimbabwe/Zambia is the world's largest by combined width and volume (~1,700m wide). Iguazu (Brazil/Argentina) is the widest waterfall system.

question: Approximately how many languages go extinct every year?
explain: Linguists estimate ~25-30 languages go extinct every year — that's roughly one every two weeks. Of the ~7,000 languages spoken today, about half are endangered. By 2100, up to 90% could disappear. When a language dies, unique knowledge about plants, navigation, ecology and human cognition is lost forever. Half of all living languages have fewer than 10,000 speakers.

question: If Antarctica's ice sheet melted completely, global sea levels would rise by about 60 meters.
explain: Antarctica holds ~26.5 million km³ of ice — enough to raise global sea levels by ~58-60 meters if it all melted. This would submerge London, New York, Tokyo, Shanghai and most coastal cities. Current projections estimate 1-2 meters of sea level rise by 2100, which would already displace hundreds of millions. The ice sheet loses ~150 billion tonnes per year currently.

question: Which country has the highest organ donation rate in the world?
explain: Spain has the world's highest organ donation rate at ~49 donors per million people (2024) — consistently #1 for 30+ years. The key: Spain uses 'opt-out' consent (everyone is a donor unless they explicitly refuse). Belgium, France and Portugal use similar systems with high rates. The USA uses opt-in — only 58% of Americans are registered donors despite 90% supporting donation.

question: In which country were the ancient Olympic Games held?
explain: The ancient Olympic Games were held at Olympia, Greece, starting in 776 BC — every 4 years for ~1,200 years. They were banned by Roman Emperor Theodosius I in 393 AD for being pagan. The modern Games were revived in Athens in 1896. During ancient Olympics, warring Greek city-states declared a sacred truce — the 'Olympic Peace' — to allow safe travel to the games.

question: Which country mines the most gold per year?
explain: China mines ~370 tonnes of gold per year — the most of any country. Australia (320t), Russia (310t) and Canada (200t) follow. South Africa, which dominated gold mining through much of the 20th century, has fallen to ~~90t as its mines deplete. All the gold ever mined in human history would fit into a cube of about 21 meters per side.

question: Which country generates the highest percentage of its electricity from wind power?
explain: Denmark generates ~55% of its electricity from wind — the highest share of any country. On windy days it produces 100%+ of demand and exports the surplus to Germany and Sweden. Denmark has been a wind pioneer since the 1970s — the Danish company Vestas is the world's largest wind turbine manufacturer. The country aims for 100% renewable electricity by 2030.

question: Which country has the most national parks?
explain: The USA has 63 designated National Parks plus 400+ protected areas in the National Park System — the most of any country. The USA also created the world's first national park: Yellowstone in 1872. Australia has ~685 national parks by count. Yellowstone sits atop a supervolcano that last erupted ~640,000 years ago and is still geologically active.

question: Which country has won the most Nobel Prizes per capita?
explain: Saint Lucia (population ~185,000) has 2 Nobel laureates — economist Arthur Lewis (1979) and poet Derek Walcott (1992) — giving it the highest Nobel rate per capita of any country. Switzerland and Sweden rank highly among large nations. Sweden benefits from hosting Nobel committees — Swedish laureates include dynamite inventor Alfred Nobel himself.

question: What is the world's most expensive coffee, made from beans passed through an animal?
explain: Black Ivory Coffee ($1,500/kg) from Thailand is made from Arabica beans eaten and excreted by elephants — the most expensive in the world. Kopi Luwak ($700/kg) from Indonesia uses civet cats and is more famous. Elephants' digestive systems break down proteins differently, reducing bitterness. Black Ivory produces only ~150 kg/year — it takes 33 kg of raw coffee cherries to make 1 kg of finished product.

question: Which country had the world's largest economy for most of human history (until ~1820)?
explain: China had the world's largest economy for most of the past 2,000 years (Angus Maddison historical GDP estimates). In 1820, China and India together produced ~50% of global GDP. China's share collapsed after the Opium Wars and internal conflicts. It only regained the #1 spot (by PPP) around 2017. The Industrial Revolution in Europe temporarily shifted global economic power westward.

question: Which is the most consumed fish in the world?
explain: Tuna is the most consumed fish globally — ~6 million tonnes caught annually. Bluefin tuna is the most prized: a single fish sold for $3.1 million at Tokyo's Tsukiji market in 2019. 70% of all tuna comes from the Pacific Ocean. Global tuna stocks have declined by 40-60% since industrial fishing began. Pollock is actually the most caught whitefish — it goes into fish sticks and McDonald's Filet-O-Fish.

question: Dust from the Sahara Desert fertilizes the Amazon rainforest.
explain: Every year, ~27 million tonnes of Saharan dust cross the Atlantic and land on the Amazon — delivering phosphorus and other nutrients that are washed away by rain. The Amazon soil is naturally poor in minerals; Saharan dust is essential to its fertility. The Bodélé Depression in Chad is the world's largest source of this dust, providing ~56% of the Amazon's phosphorus supply. The Sahara and Amazon are literally connected.

question: Which sport has the most participants worldwide?
explain: Football (soccer) has ~4 billion fans and ~265 million active players worldwide — by far the most popular sport. Cricket has ~2.5 billion fans (huge in India, Pakistan, Australia, England). Basketball has ~450 million players. The FIFA World Cup final draws ~1.5 billion viewers — the most watched sporting event on Earth.

question: Viking warriors wore horned helmets in battle.
explain: No historical evidence supports horned Viking helmets in battle. Only one Viking-age horned helmet has ever been found — a ceremonial item from Veksø, Denmark, likely used in rituals, not combat. The horned helmet image was popularized by a 19th-century Swedish artist Gustav Malmström and later spread by opera and Hollywood. Real Viking helmets were simple iron or leather domes.

question: Which country has installed the most solar power capacity in the world? (2025)
explain: China has ~700 GW of installed solar capacity (2025) — more than the rest of the world combined. It builds more solar panels per year than the entire world's capacity just 5 years ago. China also manufactures ~80% of the world's solar panels. Despite this, coal still provides ~60% of China's electricity. The USA (~180 GW) and India (~175 GW) are distant 2nd and 3rd.

question: France used the guillotine as a method of execution until the 1970s.
explain: The last guillotine execution in France took place on September 10, 1977 — Hamida Djandoubi was the last person guillotined. France abolished the death penalty in 1981 under President Mitterrand. The guillotine was introduced in 1792 as a more 'humane' and egalitarian execution method — previously, beheading by sword was reserved for nobles. Over 2,700 people were guillotined during the French Revolution's Reign of Terror.

question: Which city has the most hotel rooms in the world?
explain: Orlando, Florida has ~130,000+ hotel rooms — the most of any city in the world. This is driven by its proximity to Walt Disney World, Universal Studios, SeaWorld and 75+ other theme parks. Las Vegas (~155,000 rooms if you include the metro area) rivals it. Disney World alone covers 110 km² — twice the size of Manhattan — and hosts ~58 million visitors per year.

question: Christopher Columbus died believing he had reached Asia, never knowing he had 'discovered' a new continent.
explain: Columbus made 4 voyages to the Americas (1492-1504) and consistently believed he had reached the Indies (Asia). He died in 1506 still insisting this. It was Amerigo Vespucci who correctly argued these were previously unknown continents — published in his 1503 letter 'Mundus Novus' (New World). The continents were named 'America' after Vespucci, not Columbus.

question: Which is the busiest land border crossing in the world?
explain: The San Diego-Tijuana crossing (San Ysidro port of entry) is the busiest land border crossing on Earth with ~100,000 people and 50,000 vehicles crossing daily — ~36 million crossings per year. Tijuana and San Diego form one of the largest binational metropolitan areas in the world. Despite being the busiest, it's also heavily controlled — a stark contrast to Europe's open Schengen borders.

question: Which US state has the most active volcanoes?
explain: Alaska has ~141 potentially active volcanoes — more than any other US state and about 80% of all active US volcanoes. The Aleutian Islands chain is essentially a volcanic arc formed by the Pacific Plate subducting under North America. Hawaii has the most famous volcanoes (Kilauea, Mauna Loa) but fewer in total. The USA as a whole has ~169 active volcanoes.

question: What percentage of Thailand's male population becomes a Buddhist monk at some point in their life?
explain: Over 80% of Thai men spend time as Buddhist monks — even if only for a few weeks or months. It's considered a rite of passage and a way to make merit for their families. Thailand has ~41,000 Buddhist temples (wats) and ~300,000 monks. Even Thai kings have traditionally ordained as monks. The practice is deeply embedded in Thai cultural identity.

question: Japan has more vending machines per capita than any other country, selling unusual items like fresh eggs and umbrellas.
explain: Japan has ~5 million vending machines — 1 per 23 people. They sell ramen, pizza, fresh flowers, sake, ties, insects, used schoolgirl uniforms (controversial), live crabs and even Toyota car parts. Japan's vending culture thrives because crime is very low (machines rarely vandalized) and convenience is a core cultural value. Annual vending machine sales exceed $60 billion.

question: Singapore's GDP per capita in 1965 when it became independent was similar to which country?
explain: When Singapore became independent in 1965, its GDP per capita (~$500/year) was similar to Ghana's. Today Singapore's GDP per capita is ~$88,000 — one of the world's highest — while Ghana's is ~$2,300. Singapore achieved this in just 60 years through education, rule of law, free trade and zero tolerance for corruption. It's one of history's most remarkable economic transformations.

question: Kenya and Ethiopia together have won over 50% of all Olympic marathon medals since 1980.
explain: Kenya and Ethiopia dominate long-distance running — together winning ~70% of Olympic marathon and 10,000m medals since 1988. Kenya's Kalenjin tribe (only 0.06% of world population) has won more Olympic running medals than any other ethnic group. High-altitude training at 2,400m+ in the Rift Valley, genetics and a culture of running from childhood are cited as key factors.

question: Norway's sovereign wealth fund is the largest in the world. Roughly how much does it hold per Norwegian citizen?
explain: Norway's Government Pension Fund Global holds ~$1.7 trillion (2025) for 5.5 million citizens — about $300,000 per person. It owns ~1.5% of all listed companies globally. The fund was created in 1990 to invest oil revenues for future generations. Norway produces ~2 million barrels of oil per day but invests almost all profits abroad to avoid economic overheating.

question: Brazil is the only Portuguese-speaking country in South America.
explain: Brazil is indeed the only Portuguese-speaking country in South America — surrounded by 9 Spanish-speaking neighbors. It covers ~47% of South America's landmass. Brazil was colonized by Portugal after the Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) divided the 'New World' between Spain and Portugal along a meridian line. The Portuguese boundary happened to include what is now Brazil.

question: Iceland has a unique app to prevent accidental incest. Why is this necessary?
choices: Population is only ~380,000 and most are related
explain: Iceland's population of ~380,000 means many people are distantly related without knowing it. The 'Íslendingabók' (Book of Icelanders) app lets Icelanders check their family connection before dating — the slogan was 'bump phones before you bump in bed.' 97% of Icelanders can trace their ancestry to the same Viking settlers from 874 AD. The entire population fits in a medium-sized city.

question: Which country is the most visited tourist destination in Asia?
explain: Thailand welcomed ~39 million tourists in 2024 — the most in Southeast Asia and among the top 10 globally. Bangkok is the world's most visited city for several years running. Tourism accounts for ~20% of Thailand's GDP. The country's combination of beaches, temples, food and affordability makes it the most accessible destination in Asia for international visitors.

question: At its narrowest point, Africa and Europe are separated by less than 15 km.
explain: The Strait of Gibraltar between Morocco and Spain is only 14.4 km wide at its narrowest point — you can see Spain from Morocco on a clear day. Despite this short distance, it's one of the world's most dangerous migration crossings. Over 2,000 people attempt the crossing each month; hundreds die annually. The strait sees ~300 ships per day — one of the busiest shipping lanes in the world.

question: In what decade did Dubai begin its transformation from a small fishing village to a global city?
explain: Dubai discovered oil in 1966 and began rapid modernization in the late 1960s-70s. In 1960, Dubai had a population of ~40,000 and no paved roads. Today it has ~3.5 million residents and the world's tallest building. Ruler Sheikh Rashid famously said: 'My grandfather rode a camel, my father rode a camel, I drive a Mercedes, my son drives a Land Rover, his son will drive a Land Rover, but his son will ride a camel.'

question: French is an official language of Canada.
explain: Canada has 2 official languages: English and French. ~22% of Canadians (8 million people) speak French as their first language — mostly in Quebec. The province of Quebec is 80% French-speaking and has its own distinct civil law system based on French law. Canada's French heritage dates to 1534 when Jacques Cartier claimed the territory for France.

question: Machu Picchu in Peru sits at what altitude?
explain: Machu Picchu sits at 2,430 meters above sea level — high enough that many visitors experience altitude sickness. Built by the Inca in the 15th century, it was 'rediscovered' by Hiram Bingham in 1911 (though local people always knew of it). It covers 32,592 hectares, has ~200 structures and was home to ~750 people. Peru limits visitors to 5,000/day to protect the site.

question: Australia has more sheep than people.
explain: Australia has ~65 million sheep and ~27 million people — roughly 2.4 sheep per person. However this ratio has fallen dramatically: in 1990 there were 170 million sheep (7 per person). Australia is the world's largest wool exporter, producing ~25% of global supply. Merino wool from Australia is considered the finest in the world.

question: Colombia is considered the world's most biodiverse country per km². What makes it unique?
choices: It has the most species of birds in the world
It has more plant species than all of Europe
It has the most freshwater fish species
explain: Colombia has ~1,900 bird species — more than any country on Earth (20% of all bird species). It also has ~40,000 plant species — more than all of Europe combined. Despite covering only 0.77% of Earth's land, Colombia contains ~10% of all species. Its location bridging North and South America, with Pacific and Atlantic coasts and Andes mountains, creates extreme habitat diversity.

question: New Zealand had no land mammals before humans arrived — only birds.
explain: New Zealand separated from the supercontinent Gondwana ~80 million years ago — before most mammals evolved — so only bats (which flew there) existed as native land mammals. Birds evolved to fill mammal niches: the flightless Moa (3 meters tall, now extinct) grazed like cattle, the Kiwi forages like a badger. The introduction of rats, stoats and possums by humans has devastated native bird populations.

question: The 1815 eruption of Mount Tambora in Indonesia caused which global catastrophe?
explain: Mount Tambora's 1815 eruption (VEI 7 — the largest in recorded history) ejected so much ash and sulfur dioxide into the stratosphere that it caused global temperatures to drop by 0.4-0.7°C. 1816 became the 'Year Without a Summer' — crops failed across Europe and North America, causing widespread famine. ~71,000 people died directly; hundreds of thousands more died from starvation globally.

question: Switzerland is not a member of the European Union despite being surrounded by EU countries.
explain: Switzerland is surrounded by EU members (Germany, France, Italy, Austria, Liechtenstein) but has consistently voted to remain outside the EU — most recently in a 1992 referendum. Switzerland has bilateral agreements with the EU for trade and free movement. It's also not in the Eurozone — it uses the Swiss Franc (CHF). Switzerland is however in the Schengen Area (passport-free travel zone).

question: How much beef does the average Argentine consume per year — the highest in the world?
explain: Argentines consume ~50 kg of beef per person per year — the highest in the world. The asado (barbecue) is a national institution and social ritual. Argentina has ~54 million cattle for 46 million people (more cattle than people). Argentine beef is renowned globally — the Pampas grasslands provide ideal grazing. Steak restaurants (parrillas) are on virtually every block in Buenos Aires.

question: Over 90% of Egypt's population lives on less than 5% of its land area.
explain: 95% of Egypt is desert — the Sahara and Eastern Desert. ~95% of Egyptians live along the Nile River and its delta, which covers just ~4% of the country's area. Cairo alone has ~21 million people, making it Africa's largest city. The Nile Delta is one of the world's most densely populated agricultural zones. Egypt's population has grown from 20 million in 1950 to ~110 million today.

question: Japan has approximately how many convenience stores (konbini) for 125 million people?
explain: Japan has ~56,000 convenience stores — roughly 1 per 2,200 people. Chains like 7-Eleven (founded in the USA but now 40% Japan-owned), Lawson and FamilyMart are open 24/7 and sell fresh hot food, pay bills, print documents, buy concert tickets and even offer ATMs. Japanese konbini food quality is so high that Michelin-starred chefs have praised it. 7-Eleven Japan has more stores than 7-Eleven USA.

question: Russia is so large that it spans both Europe and Asia.
explain: Russia covers 17.1 million km² — the world's largest country, spanning 11 time zones across Europe and Asia. The Ural Mountains traditionally mark the boundary. ~77% of Russia's territory is in Asia (Siberia) but ~75% of its population lives in the European part (west of the Urals). Russia is larger than Pluto's surface area (16.6 million km²) and nearly twice the size of the USA or China.

question: What is the national sport of Senegal, whose top athletes are celebrities earning millions?
explain: Laamb (Senegalese wrestling) is the national sport — traditional wrestlers are among the country's biggest celebrities. Top champions earn millions of CFA francs per fight and have huge social media followings. Matches combine wrestling with pre-fight rituals involving griots (praise singers) and marabouts (Islamic holy men) performing spiritual protection ceremonies. Stadiums sell out for major fights.

question: Cows are sacred in India and it is illegal to slaughter them in most Indian states.
explain: Cows are revered in Hinduism as symbols of abundance and non-violence. 29 of India's 36 states and territories have laws restricting or banning cow slaughter.
